title: SEGMENT 9 — Drosophila in the molecular era: homeobox, P-elements, and UAS-Gal4

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Drosophila in the molecular era: homeobox, P-elements, and UAS-Gal4
For a long time — from Morgan in 1908 through the 1970s — fly genetics was almost entirely a phenotype-and-mapping discipline.
You could find mutants, map them, draw inferences about what genes did.
But you couldn't really get inside the gene at the molecular level.
You couldn't clone a fly gene, sequence it, or move it around.

The transition from classical fly genetics to molecular fly genetics happened in the late 1970s and early 1980s, and it involved a series of techniques that, together, turned the fly into one of the most powerful experimental platforms in molecular biology.
I want to highlight three of these techniques, because they remain central to fly research today.
Technique one: the discovery of the homeobox.
In the 1940s, a series of bizarre mutations had been described in flies — mutations called Antennapedia, Bithorax, and others — in which body parts grew in the wrong places.
Antennapedia flies have legs growing out of their heads, where the antennae should be.
Bithorax flies have four wings instead of two — the third thoracic segment, normally bearing balance organs, has been transformed into a copy of the second thoracic segment, which bears the wings.
These were called homeotic mutations — mutations that transform one body part into another.
They suggested, in the genetic terms of the era, that there were master regulators specifying which segment of the fly grew which appendages.

In 1983 and 1984, two groups — Walter Gehring's lab at Basel and Matthew Scott and Amy Weiner at Indiana — cloned the homeotic genes and discovered that they all contained a 180-base-pair sequence in common, encoding a 60-amino-acid protein domain that came to be called the homeodomain, encoded by what was named the homeobox.
The homeodomain turned out to be a DNA-binding domain — these were transcription factors that bound to specific DNA sequences and turned on or off the genes that built each body segment.
Then came the spectacular cross-species result.
When the homeobox sequences were used as probes to look for related genes in mice and humans, the searchers found that mammals have the same genes.

Mammalian homeobox genes are arranged in the genome in the same linear order as the fly homeotic genes, expressed along the body axis in the same order, and doing the same job — specifying which body segment grows which structures.
The Hox genes are conserved from fly to human.
The molecular logic of the vertebrate body plan was, deep down, the molecular logic of the fly body plan.

Walter Gehring's lab even showed, much later, that the master eye-development gene of the fly — eyeless — could induce eye formation in a mouse, and the master eye gene of the mouse — Pax6 — could induce eye formation in a fly.
The conservation went all the way down.
This was the moment fly genetics convinced everyone, even the skeptics, that what we learn in flies translates to vertebrates.
The Hox discovery is the single most powerful piece of evidence for the model-organism bet.
The genes that pattern your body are the genes that pattern a fly's body.
They have the same names, the same chromosomal arrangement, the same job.
Technique two: P-element transposition.
In the late 1970s, geneticists noticed that certain fly crosses produced a burst of mutations in the offspring — a phenomenon called hybrid dysgenesis.

It turned out to be caused by a class of mobile DNA elements called P elements — transposons that could jump around the fly genome, inserting themselves at new locations and disrupting whichever gene they landed in.
A natural reaction to discovering a mutation-causing parasite might be: damn, this is annoying, let's get rid of it.
Allan Spradling and Gerald Rubin had the opposite reaction.
They realized that a controllable transposon is exactly the tool you want for inserting DNA at random into a genome.

In 1982, they showed that you could engineer P elements — strip them of their natural ability to jump on their own, and put in a separately encoded transposase that you could supply or withhold at will — to create a system where you could put any gene you wanted into a P element, inject the P element into fly embryos along with the transposase enzyme, and have the P element insert your gene into the fly's genome.
This was the first reliable method for making transgenic flies.
Suddenly you could put any cloned gene into a fly, with regulatory sequences of your choice, and ask how it behaved.
You could overexpress genes.
You could put in marked versions of genes to track where they were expressed.
You could make collections of random insertion lines — "enhancer trap" libraries — to discover new genes by where they landed.
For a decade, P-element transgenesis was the workhorse of molecular fly genetics.

It has since been supplemented by more refined methods — site-specific integration using bacteriophage integrases like phiC31, CRISPR-based insertion — but the original P-element system is what put the fly on a level molecular playing field with bacterial and yeast genetics.
Technique three: the UAS-Gal4 binary system.
This one is so beautiful that it deserves its own paragraph.

In 1993, Andrea Brand and Norbert Perrimon adapted a yeast transcription system for use in flies, and the result is what's now called the UAS-Gal4 binary system — the genetic toolkit that defines modern fly research.
Here's the logic.
Yeast has a transcription factor called Gal4, and Gal4 binds to a specific DNA sequence called the UAS — Upstream Activating Sequence.
When Gal4 is present, it binds UAS and turns on whatever gene is downstream.
When Gal4 is absent, nothing happens.
The system is exquisitely specific — Gal4 doesn't interact with any normal fly DNA, and UAS sequences don't interact with any normal fly transcription factor.
Brand and Perrimon split the system in two.
They built driver lines: fly strains that express Gal4 under the control of a tissue-specific or stage-specific promoter.
So a "neuronal Gal4" driver line expresses Gal4 only in neurons.
An "eye Gal4" driver line expresses Gal4 only in the developing eye.
A "heart Gal4" driver line expresses Gal4 only in the heart.
There are now thousands of driver lines, covering essentially every cell type and developmental stage in the fly.
Then they built responder lines: fly strains that have UAS sites followed by whatever gene you want to express.
UAS-geneX.
The responder line, by itself, doesn't express geneX, because there's no Gal4 around to activate it.
When you cross a driver line to a responder line, the offspring carry both the Gal4 and the UAS-geneX.
In any tissue where the driver expresses Gal4, the UAS becomes active and geneX gets expressed.
In every other tissue, geneX stays off.
So you can express any gene in any tissue at any time, just by picking the right driver and the right responder and crossing them.
The analogy: the UAS-Gal4 binary system is the fly genetic toolkit — drive any gene in any tissue at any time using two crosses.
Want to know what happens when you overexpress tau specifically in motor neurons?
Cross motor-neuron-Gal4 to UAS-tau.
Want to silence a gene specifically in the eye?
Cross eye-Gal4 to UAS-shRNA-against-that-gene.
Want to activate neurons by light?
Cross neuron-Gal4 to UAS-channelrhodopsin.
You can mix and match drivers and responders to assemble essentially any genetic experiment in a few weeks.
This kind of cellular-level genetic manipulation is far harder in mouse or fish.
In flies it's routine.
Every fly geneticist has a list of preferred drivers and a list of responders and combines them like Lego.
The system has been extended with temperature-sensitive variants, drug-inducible variants, intersectional logic that requires the simultaneous presence of two drivers — every conceivable refinement.
The fly is, today, the easiest animal in which to do precise spatial-temporal genetic experiments.
Not because the fly biology is uniquely amenable, but because forty years of accumulated tool-building have made it so.

The big idea to leave this segment with: the fly went from a classical-genetics organism to a fully modern molecular platform through a series of technical revolutions — homeobox conservation, P-element transposition, UAS-Gal4 binary expression — that together turned every fly experiment into a precise molecular intervention.
A modern fly paper looks nothing like a 1925 Morgan paper.
Same animal, completely different toolset.
And every piece of that toolset is itself a product of the model-organism community — built by fly biologists, for fly biologists, on a foundation of shared resources and shared techniques.
